request.question: Marcus Chong played Huey P. Newton in a film directed by Mario Van Peebles in what year?

title: Panther (film)
Posse (1993 film)
Marcus Chong
New Jack City
Love Kills (film)
Mario's Green House
A Huey P. Newton Story
Red Sky (2014 film)
Rappin'
Baadasssss!
sentences: Panther is a 1995 film directed by Mario Van Peebles, from a screenplay adapted by his father, Melvin Van Peebles, from his novel of the same name.
The film portrays the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense, tracing the organization from its founding through its decline in a compressed timeframe.
Creative license is taken but the general trajectory of the Party and its experiences is factual.
Posse is a 1993 American revisionist Western film directed by and starring Mario Van Peebles.
Featuring a large ensemble cast, the film tells the story of a posse of African-American soldiers and one ostracized white soldier, who are all betrayed by a corrupt colonel.
The story starts with the group escaping with a cache of gold, and continues with their leader Jessie Lee (Van Peebles) taking revenge on the men who killed his preacher father.
The story is presented as a flashback told by an unnamed old man (Woody Strode).
The title of the film refers to a group of people who are summoned to help law enforcement officers.
Marcus Chong (born Marcus Wyatt; July 8, 1967) is an American actor.
His best-known roles are Huey P. Newton in "Panther" (1995), directed by Mario Van Peebles, and Tank the Operator in "The Matrix" (1999).

New Jack City is a 1991 American action gangster film based upon an original story and screenplay by Thomas Lee Wright, and directed by Mario Van Peebles in his directorial debut, who also co-stars in the film.
The film stars Wesley Snipes, Ice-T, Allen Payne, Chris Rock, Mario Van Peebles and Judd Nelson.
The film was released in the United States on March 8, 1991.
Love Kills is a 1998 film by Mario Van Peebles starring Mario Van Peebles, Lesley Ann Warren, and Daniel Baldwin.
Mario's Green House was an 8-episode 2009 television series on TV One starring Mario Van Peebles.
The series was an environmentally conscious reality television show, in which Van Peebles made his house 'greener'.
The series also starred Melvin Van Peebles, who required his own room for entertaining female guests.
Ed Begley, Jr. was involved in the production.
A Huey P. Newton Story (2001) is an American film adaptation directed by Spike Lee.
The movie was created, written and performed, as a solo performance, by Roger Guenveur Smith at The Joseph Papp Public Theater.
In this performance, Smith creates a representation of the activist Huey P. Newton’s life and time as a person, a citizen and an activist.
During the performance, images are shown up-stage from activist movement era.
The simple arrangement of Smith sitting in a chair stage-center makes the audience focus on the dialogue of the performer.
Smith captures the attention of the audience throughout the film by putting into play his solo performance skills.
Smith's idea for the performance originated in 1989 and took root as a stage play in 1996.
Smith's performance attempt to show a shy individual that Huey P. Newton believed himself to be.
He did not consider himself a charismatic person, although he had made many contributions to his community.
Smith shows Newton as a conservative individual who is disgusted by having microphones and cameras close to him.
Red Sky (previously known as Kerosene Cowboys) is a 2014 straight-to-DVD action/thriller film directed by Mario Van Peebles and starring Cam Gigandet, Shane West and Rachael Leigh Cook.

Adam Prince and Van Peebles wrote the screenplay based on a story by Nikolay Suslov and Dave Riggs, who in turn based the story on characters from the novel "Kerosene Cowboys: Manning the Spare" by Randy Arrington.
It is a Russian-American co-production (Svarog Films and Afterburner Films), supported by both Russian and American military forces.
The film has been described as a ""Top Gun"–style pic" about pilots of an elite naval attack squadron.
Rappin' is a 1985 film directed by Joel Silberg, written by Adam Friedman and Robert J. Litz, produced by Menahem Golan and Yoram Globus and starring Mario Van Peebles.
The film is a sequel to "", and is also known as Breakdance 3: Electric Boogaloo.

Although it features Ice-T (who featured in "Breakin'" and "Breakin' 2: Electric Boogaloo"), "Rappin<nowiki>'</nowiki>" has a plot unconnected to the previous two films and features different lead characters and locations.
The film has developed a small cult following, despite Van Peebles' lack of rapping ability and the film's overall poor production values.
Baadasssss!
is a 2003 American biopic, written, produced, directed by, and starring Mario Van Peebles.

The film is based on the struggles of Van Peebles' father Melvin Van Peebles (played by Mario himself), as he attempts to film and distribute "Sweet Sweetback's Baadasssss Song", a film that was widely credited with showing Hollywood that a viable African-American audience existed, and thus influencing the creation of the Blaxploitation genre.
The film also stars Joy Bryant, Nia Long, Ossie Davis, Paul Rodriguez, Rainn Wilson, and Terry Crews.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Marcus Chong played Huey P. Newton in a film directed by Mario Van Peebles in what year?
hop1_docs: Marcus Chong | Marcus Chong (born Marcus Wyatt; July 8, 1967) is an American actor.  His best-known roles are Huey P. Newton in "Panther" (1995), directed by Mario Van Peebles, and Tank the Operator in "The Matrix" (1999).

Mario Van Peebles | Mario Van Peebles (born 1957) is an American film director and actor best known for directing "New Jack City" in 1991.  He is the son of actor and filmmaker Melvin Van Peebles, whom he portrayed in the 2003 biopic "Baadasssss! " that he also co-wrote and directed.

Baadasssss! | Baadasssss!  is a 2003 American biopic, written, produced, directed by, and starring Mario Van Peebles.  The film is based on the struggles of Van Peebles' father Melvin Van Peebles (played by Mario himself), as he attempts to film and distribute "Sweet Sweetback's Baadasssss Song", a film that was widely credited with showing Hollywood that a viable African-American audience existed, and thus influencing the creation of the Blaxploitation genre.  The film also stars Joy Bryant, Nia Long, Ossie Davis, Paul Rodriguez, Rainn Wilson, and Terry Crews.
Love Kills (film) | Love Kills is a 1998 film by Mario Van Peebles starring Mario Van Peebles, Lesley Ann Warren, and Daniel Baldwin.

New Jack City | New Jack City is a 1991 American action gangster film based upon an original story and screenplay by Thomas Lee Wright, and directed by Mario Van Peebles in his directorial debut, who also co-stars in the film.  The film stars Wesley Snipes, Ice-T, Allen Payne, Chris Rock, Mario Van Peebles and Judd Nelson.  The film was released in the United States on March 8, 1991.

Identity Crisis (film) | Identity Crisis is a 1989 comedy film directed by Melvin Van Peebles.  Written by Mario Van Peebles, the film is about a rapper who winds up sharing his body with the soul of a dead fashion designer, switching between personalities every time he is struck on the head.  It was released direct-to-video in 1991.

Gang in Blue | Gang in Blue is a 1996 American film co-directed by Melvin Van Peebles and his son, Mario Van Peebles, about a black police officer who discovers a cell of white supremacist vigilantes within his department.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Marcus Chong — [1]: "Marcus Chong (born Marcus Wyatt; July 8, 1967)"
- Huey P. Newton — [1]: "Huey P. Newton in "Panther" (1995)"
- "Panther" — [1]: ""Panther" (1995)"
- 1995 — [1]: "(1995)"
- Mario Van Peebles — [1]: "directed by Mario Van Peebles"

Ambiguities:
- None found; Marcus Chong, the role Huey P. Newton, the film "Panther", and the year "(1995)" are consistently linked in [1].

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing relation: the year Mario Van Peebles directed the film in which Marcus Chong played Huey P. Newton is explicitly given as 1995.

Retrieval direction:
- Confirm primary source pages: "Marcus Chong" and "Panther (1995 film)"
- Optionally fetch "Mario Van Peebles" for director filmography verification
- Final answer: 1995.
hop2_docs: Marcus Chong | Marcus Chong (born Marcus Wyatt; July 8, 1967) is an American actor.  His best-known roles are Huey P. Newton in "Panther" (1995), directed by Mario Van Peebles, and Tank the Operator in "The Matrix" (1999).

Lin Chong | Lin Chong is a fictional character in "Water Margin", one of the Four Great Classical Novels of Chinese literature.  He ranks 6th of the 36 Heavenly Spirits of the 108 Liangshan heroes and is nicknamed "Panther Head".  In the world of published fiction, he is a former student of Zhou Tong.

Ping Chong | Ping Chong (; born Toronto, Ontario, Canada, 1946) is an American contemporary theater director, choreographer, video and installation artist.  He was born in Toronto and raised in the Chinatown section of New York City.  Chong is internationally recognized as a director, writer, and multi-disciplinary artist, and is considered a seminal figure in Asian American theatre and the Asian American arts movement.

Elton Chong | Elton Chong (real name Jeong Jin Hwa) was born in 1955 and is a Korean martial artist, action director, and actor.  Elton Chong has mostly worked in South Korea with actors such as Eagle Han-ying, Casanova Wong, Michael Wong and Dragon Lee.  While most of his work is with the director Kim Jeong Yong, Elton Chong often appeared in Godfrey Ho's movies, especially Hong Kong movies that were imported into Korea.  He is perhaps best known for the movie "Shaolin Drunken Monkey" (known in Korea as "The Shaolin Chief Cook".) .
Chong Wai-kin | Chong Wai-kin is a Hong Kong television producer and director.
Felix Chong | Felix Chong Man-Keung () (born January 1, 1968 in Hong Kong) is a Hong Kong screenwriter, film director and actor.

Marcus Lucas | Marcus Lucas (born March 1, 1992) is an American football tight end for the Indianapolis Colts of the National Football League (NFL).  He was signed by the Carolina Panthers as an undrafted free agent in 2014.  He played college football at Missouri.
response.summary_2: Marcus Chong played Huey P. Newton in Mario Van Peebles' film Panther (1995).
